questions.stem: Adaptations for movement are described as:

options.text: A universal feature of living organisms
Absent in all plants and microbes

questions.explanation: Adaptations for movement are a universal feature of living organisms, explored using both motile and sessile species.

questions.scenario: A sessile organism stays fixed in one place.
questions.stem: This means it:

options.text: Does not move from place to place as an adult

questions.explanation: Sessile organisms are fixed in place; comparing them with motile species illustrates the concept of movement.

questions.stem: According to the sliding filament model, muscle contraction occurs when:

options.text: Actin and myosin filaments slide past each other

questions.explanation: In the sliding filament model, actin and myosin filaments slide past one another, shortening the sarcomere.

questions.stem: What happens to its length?
questions.explanation: As the filaments slide, the sarcomere shortens during contraction.

questions.stem: The two main filament proteins involved in the sliding filament model are:
questions.explanation: Actin (thin) and myosin (thick) filaments slide past each other during contraction.

questions.stem: The role of the protein titin is to:

options.text: Help sarcomeres recoil and prevent overstretching

questions.explanation: The huge protein titin helps sarcomeres recoil after stretching and prevents them from overstretching.

questions.scenario: Muscle tissue can only exert force when it contracts, not when it relaxes.
questions.stem: Why are antagonistic muscles needed?

options.text: One muscle's contraction is needed to reverse another's movement
Muscles can push as well as pull
A single muscle can move a joint both ways

questions.explanation: Because muscles only pull (contract), antagonistic pairs are needed so that one can reverse the movement caused by the other.

questions.stem: A motor unit in skeletal muscle consists of:

options.text: A motor neuron and the muscle fibres it controls

questions.explanation: A motor unit includes a motor neuron, the muscle fibres it stimulates and the neuromuscular junctions connecting them.

questions.stem: The connection between a motor neuron and a muscle fibre is called the:
questions.explanation: The neuromuscular junction connects a motor neuron to the muscle fibres it controls.

questions.stem: Skeletons act as anchorage for muscles and as:
questions.explanation: Skeletons provide anchorage for muscles and act as levers to produce movement.

questions.stem: Arthropods have exoskeletons, whereas vertebrates have:
questions.explanation: Arthropods have exoskeletons; vertebrates have internal endoskeletons.

questions.scenario: The human hip is used as an example of a synovial joint.
questions.stem: Which structure reduces friction within a synovial joint?
questions.explanation: Synovial fluid lubricates the joint, reducing friction; cartilage also cushions the bone ends.

questions.stem: At a synovial joint, what connects muscle to bone?
questions.explanation: Tendons connect muscle to bone; ligaments connect bone to bone.

questions.stem: In the human hip joint, students should be able to name which two bones?
questions.explanation: For the hip joint, students should be able to name the femur and the pelvis.

questions.stem: At a synovial joint, ligaments function to:

options.text: Connect bone to bone and stabilize the joint

questions.explanation: Ligaments connect bone to bone, helping to stabilize the joint.

questions.scenario: A student measures how far a joint can move in different directions.
questions.stem: This is measuring the joint's:
questions.explanation: Range of motion is how far a joint can move; it can be compared across several dimensions.

questions.stem: Which instrument is used to measure joint angles?
questions.explanation: A goniometer (or computer analysis of images) is used to measure joint angles when assessing range of motion.

questions.scenario: The internal and external intercostal muscles work as an antagonistic pair.
questions.stem: This means they:

options.text: Move the ribcage in opposite directions
Have no effect on the ribcage

questions.explanation: The differently oriented internal and external intercostals move the ribcage in opposite directions, an antagonistic action.

questions.scenario: One layer of intercostal muscle contracts.
questions.stem: What happens to the other layer?

options.text: It is stretched, storing potential energy in titin

questions.explanation: When one intercostal layer contracts, it stretches the other, storing potential energy in the protein titin.

questions.stem: Which is a reason animals use locomotion?
questions.explanation: Reasons for locomotion include foraging for food, escaping danger, searching for a mate and migration.

questions.scenario: An animal travels a long seasonal distance to a breeding ground.
questions.stem: This reason for locomotion is:
questions.explanation: Migration is one of the reasons for locomotion, alongside foraging, escaping danger and searching for a mate.

questions.stem: Which is an adaptation for swimming in marine mammals?

options.text: A streamlined body and limbs modified into flippers

questions.explanation: Marine mammals show streamlining, flippers, a tail fluke and airway changes for periodic breathing between dives.

questions.scenario: A whale's tail moves up and down to propel it through water.
questions.stem: This tail structure is called a:
questions.explanation: The tail of marine mammals forms a fluke that moves up and down for swimming.

questions.stem: The functional contractile unit of a muscle fibre is the:
questions.explanation: The sarcomere is the functional contractile unit that shortens when actin and myosin slide past each other.

questions.stem: Titin is described as an 'immense' protein because it:

options.text: Is very large, spanning much of the sarcomere

questions.explanation: Titin is a very large protein that helps the sarcomere recoil and prevents overstretching.

questions.scenario: A single motor neuron stimulates a group of muscle fibres.
questions.stem: When the neuron fires, these fibres will:
questions.explanation: All fibres in a motor unit are stimulated by the same neuron, so they contract together.

questions.scenario: A muscle contracts and pulls on a bone, which pivots at a joint.
questions.stem: The bone is acting as a:
questions.explanation: Bones act as levers, with muscles anchored to them producing movement about a joint.

questions.stem: Cartilage at the ends of bones in a synovial joint functions to:

options.text: Cushion and reduce friction between bones

questions.explanation: Cartilage covers the bone ends, cushioning them and, with synovial fluid, reducing friction.

questions.stem: Comparing the range of motion of a joint 'in a number of dimensions' means measuring how it moves:
questions.explanation: Range of motion is compared across several dimensions (directions/planes) using tools like a goniometer.

questions.stem: The different fibre orientations of the internal and external intercostals allow them to:

options.text: Move the ribcage in opposite directions
Move the ribcage in the same direction

questions.explanation: Because their fibres run in different orientations, the two intercostal layers move the ribcage in opposite directions.

questions.stem: Studying both a motile and a sessile species helps students:

options.text: Explore the concept of movement across organisms

questions.explanation: Comparing a motile and a sessile species helps explore the range of adaptations for movement in living organisms.

questions.scenario: During contraction, do the actin and myosin filaments themselves shorten?
questions.stem: According to the sliding filament model:

options.text: No, they slide past each other without shortening

questions.explanation: The filaments do not shorten; they slide past each other, shortening the sarcomere overall.

questions.scenario: After a stretched muscle is released, the sarcomere returns to its resting length.
questions.stem: Which protein assists this recoil?
questions.explanation: Titin helps the sarcomere recoil after being stretched and prevents overstretching.

questions.stem: An advantage of an endoskeleton over an exoskeleton is that it:

options.text: Can grow with the organism without being shed

questions.explanation: An internal endoskeleton can grow with the vertebrate, whereas an exoskeleton must be moulted to allow growth.

questions.stem: Which set of structures is involved in movement at a synovial joint?

options.text: Bones, cartilage, synovial fluid, ligaments, muscles and tendons

questions.explanation: Movement at a synovial joint involves bones, cartilage, synovial fluid, ligaments, muscles and tendons.

questions.stem: The strength of a muscle contraction can be increased by recruiting:
questions.explanation: Recruiting more motor units brings more muscle fibres into action, increasing the force of contraction.

questions.scenario: An animal runs away from a predator.
questions.stem: This reason for locomotion is:
questions.explanation: Escaping from danger is one reason for locomotion, along with foraging, mating and migration.

questions.scenario: A marine mammal must breathe air but spends time underwater.
questions.stem: Which adaptation supports this lifestyle?

options.text: Changes to the airways to allow periodic breathing between dives

questions.explanation: Marine mammals have airway changes allowing periodic breathing between dives, alongside streamlining and flippers.

questions.stem: Which method could be used to measure a joint's range of motion?

options.text: Computer analysis of images or a goniometer

questions.explanation: Joint angles for range of motion are measured using computer image analysis or a goniometer.

questions.stem: The intercostal muscles are given as an example of antagonistic action facilitating:
questions.explanation: The intercostals illustrate antagonistic muscle action producing internal body movements of the ribcage.

questions.scenario: A biceps and triceps work as an antagonistic pair at the elbow.
questions.stem: To straighten the arm, the triceps contracts and the biceps:
questions.explanation: In an antagonistic pair, one muscle relaxes as the other contracts, since muscles can only pull.

questions.stem: During contraction, the region where actin and myosin overlap:
questions.explanation: As the filaments slide past one another during contraction, their overlap increases and the sarcomere shortens.

questions.stem: For a muscle to move a bone, the muscle must be:
questions.explanation: Skeletons provide anchorage for muscles so that muscle contraction can move the bones as levers.

questions.scenario: An animal moves around to find a partner for reproduction.
questions.stem: This reason for locomotion is:
questions.explanation: Searching for a mate is one of the reasons for locomotion, along with foraging, escaping danger and migration.

questions.stem: Streamlining in marine mammals is beneficial because it:
questions.explanation: A streamlined body reduces drag, making swimming more efficient for marine mammals.

questions.stem: Which components make up a motor unit?

options.text: A motor neuron, muscle fibres and neuromuscular junctions

questions.explanation: A motor unit comprises a motor neuron, the muscle fibres it controls and the neuromuscular junctions between them.

questions.stem: The human hip joint allows a wide range of movement because it is a:
questions.explanation: The hip is a synovial ball-and-socket joint, allowing a wide range of motion; students name the femur and pelvis.

questions.scenario: Potential energy is stored in titin when an intercostal layer is stretched.
questions.stem: This stored energy can help:

options.text: The muscle recoil when it later contracts or relaxes

questions.explanation: Stretching stores potential energy in titin, contributing to the recoil of the sarcomere.

questions.stem: Which pairing of animal group and skeleton type is correct?
questions.explanation: Arthropods have exoskeletons; vertebrates have endoskeletons, both anchoring muscles and acting as levers.

questions.stem: The sliding filament model explains how a muscle:

options.text: Generates force and shortens during contraction

questions.explanation: The sliding filament model explains muscle contraction as actin and myosin sliding to shorten sarcomeres and generate force.
